CLARITY.justification: Overall the paper is well-organized (Intro → dynamical analysis → experiments → conclusion) and clearly states the main contribution early (the CFN model (Eq.1–2) and its chaos-free dynamics). Notation is mostly consistent and definitions (e.g. σ, gates, Φ) and lemmas are clearly presented, and proofs/summaries are concise. Presentation issues: several duplicated/garbled paragraphs and table/text fragments near the end (repeated sentences and mangled table entries such as multiple repeated ‘‘Vanilla RNN 5M parameters...’’ lines) hinder smooth reading, and a few figure/table references appear without the figures in the provided text. Fixing these editorial problems would make the manuscript excellent.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper proposes a clearly specified new gated RNN variant (eqs. (1)–(2)) — the CFN — together with formal dynamical analysis (Lemma 1 and Lemma 2) proving it is "chaos-free" and contrasting this with demonstrated chaotic behavior in LSTM/GRU (Sec. 2.1). This is more than a minor implementation tweak: the work couples a simple architectural change with a theoretical perspective (dynamical-systems proofs) and empirical results (Sec. 3) showing comparable language-model performance. The contribution is a notable and well-differentiated novelty (architecture + theoretical claim) that would be of clear interest to experts, though it is not a radical departure from gated-RNN design principles.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper's mathematical claims about the CFN are correct: Lemma 1 follows from |tanh(x)|≤|x| and a geometric-series iteration, and Lemma 2 correctly proves global convergence to zero for the autonomous CFN (so no autonomous chaos). The demonstrations that specific LSTM/GRU instances (synthetic weights and a trained PTB LSTM) exhibit sensitive/chaotic behavior are plausible and methodologically standard (zero inputs, perturb initial states). Experimental comparisons supporting that CFN matches LSTM on PTB/Text8 are presented, but the empirical methodology has weaknesses (single-run results, no statistical variability reported, a nonstandard normalized-gradient optimizer, and differing dropout hyperparameters between models), which weakens the strength of the comparative claim. Overall the technical arguments are sound with minor empirical-methodology shortcomings that do not overturn the main conclusions.
